text: The video demonstrates a reduced port laparoscopic complete mesocolic excision and intracorporeal anastomosis for right-sided colon cancer. A single port is inserted through the umbilical incision, with an additional 5mm trocar in the right lower quadrant. The surgeon incises the peritoneum at the retrocecal recess and performs inferior to superior mobilization of the cecum and ascending colon. Monopolar cautery and ultrasonic shears are used to expose an avascular plane along Toldt's line, revealing the duodenum, pancreas, and right kidney. Lateral to medial dissection continues until the posterior side of the superior mesenteric vein is exposed. Hepatic mobilization is performed, and the visceral peritoneum anterior to the central vessels is incised for primary vascular control and DIII lymphadenectomy along the superior mesenteric vessels.

The surgeon uses a multifunctional ultrasonic device for dissection, grasping, cauterization, sealing, and cutting of vessels. Extensive lymph node dissection is performed around the superior mesenteric vessels. The ileocolic artery is divided and ligated with a hemolog clip, and the ileocolic vein is ligated and transected at its root, where it drains into the superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon identifies the branches of the middle colic artery after lymph node dissection. Bleeding from the right branch of the middle colic artery is controlled using an ultrasonic device, which securely seals the vessel without the need for a surgical clip. The final intraoperative view shows the complete mesocolic excision and central vascular ligation achieved through reduced port laparoscopic access. A side-to-side intracorporeal anastomosis is performed using an articulated laparoscopic stapler.
